setting.note: The book never names the March sisters' town, but Alcott wrote it into her own: Concord, Massachusetts, and her family home on Lexington Road. The old brown March house is drawn from Orchard House, where Alcott lived and wrote and which is now a museum; the stately stone Laurence mansion next door is drawn from the real house over the hedge; and the country-like suburb, its groves and quiet streets, is the Concord of the Alcotts, Emerson and Thoreau. Amy's later tour of Nice and Switzerland is a real excursion abroad. It brings Louisa May Alcott into the atlas.

rights.originalWork: Public domain. Louisa May Alcott, first published 1868; the author died in 1888.
rights.territoryNotes: Public domain in the United States (published 1868) and in the United Kingdom and the EU, where the author died more than 70 years ago (1888).
rights.attribution: Louisa May Alcott, Little Women (1868). Text from Project Gutenberg.

palette.note: The green of the Delectable Mountains from the Pilgrim's Progress the sisters play at, and of the Concord fields and groves that surround the March house. Clears WCAG AA on paper.
